items.correct: Yes — plants respire all the time to release energy; they also photosynthesise when there is light.
items.misconception: Plants only photosynthesise and do not respire; respiration is for animals only.
items.random_wrong: Plants only respire at night and only photosynthesise during the day; the processes never overlap.
items.why: Because photosynthesis is taught as the plant equivalent of respiration, students think it replaces respiration in plants entirely.

items.q: Where do plants get most of their biomass from?
items.correct: From carbon dioxide in the air — the carbon in plant tissue comes mostly from CO2 fixed during photosynthesis.
items.misconception: Plants get most of their mass from the soil through their roots.
items.random_wrong: Plants gain most of their mass directly from sunlight, which has weight.
items.why: Roots in soil look like the source of food/mass, but actually most plant dry mass is carbon from atmospheric CO2.

items.q: What happens to an enzyme when it is denatured?
items.correct: Its tertiary structure (3D shape) changes, particularly the active site, so it can no longer bind substrate effectively.
items.misconception: The enzyme has died because enzymes are alive.
items.random_wrong: The enzyme has been completely broken into amino acids.
items.why: Students think of enzymes as alive (probably because they are produced by living things), so they describe denaturation as death rather than shape change.

items.q: What is the compensation point of a plant?
items.correct: Light intensity where photosynthesis equals respiration
items.why: It's Light intensity where photosynthesis equals respiration. “Maximum rate of photosynthesis” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Why might burning paraffin in a glasshouse boost photosynthesis?
items.why: It's It adds CO2 and heat. “It removes CO2” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If a glasshouse strategy doubles yield from 10 kg/m^2 to a new value, what is the new value in kg/m^2?
items.why: The real figure is 20. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
